title: Microfluidic Actuation by Einstein-de Haas Spin Torque
abstract: We propose spin-current microfluidic actuation of a sealed liquid metal. Spin angular momentum injected from Pt contacts enters the liquid as an Einstein-de Haas torque and is converted through micropolar angular-momentum balance into viscous flow without pressure drive, moving walls, magnetic fields, Lorentz forces, or charge flow through the liquid. The dc velocity obeys universal spin-diffusion scaling, and the finite-frequency spin-mechanical admittance resolves viscous momentum diffusion, spin transport, microrotation relaxation, and interface transparency of the liquid-metal channel.
summary: This paper proposes a way to move liquid metal in sealed microchannels using spin torque from the Einstein-de Haas effect. It shows that the flow can be controlled by tuning spin-current symmetry and provides a method to use frequency sweeps to measure fluid and spin properties.
main_problem: Developing a method for microfluidic actuation in sealed liquid metals using the Einstein-de Haas effect and characterizing the resulting spin-mechanical response.
main_result: The authors establish a universal scaling law for DC velocity profiles based on the ratio of channel width to spin-diffusion length and demonstrate that the system acts as a frequency-dependent low-pass filter.
method: The study uses analytical derivations of micropolar hydrodynamic equations and frequency-domain analysis (admittance spectroscopy) to model both steady-state and AC responses.
model_or_system: A Pt/liquid-metal/Pt geometry where spin current injected via the Spin Hall Effect transfers angular momentum to a confined, incompressible, micropolar liquid metal.
key_observables: DC velocity profiles, net mass flux, orbital angular momentum, and AC spin-mechanical admittance (magnitude and phase).
important_parameters: Spin-diffusion length (lambda), channel width (L), kinematic viscosity (nu), and the dimensionless ratio L/lambda.
main_assumptions: Low-Reynolds-number, low-frequency, and incompressible flow; assumes an isotropic liquid and neglects micro-inertia and convective terms in the steady-state limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic; Figure 2 illustrates the time evolution of velocity profiles for odd and even modes; Figure 3 presents universal scaling of DC response and the frequency-dependent AC admittance and phase.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the EdH-driven microfluidic concept, develops the micropolar hydrodynamic model, derives steady-state scaling laws and symmetry-resolved modes, extends the theory to AC admittance spectroscopy, and discusses parameter extraction.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of the proposed spin-current actuation geometry. A conducting liquid metal is confined in a channel of width 2L between two Pt electrodes. A charge current in Pt generates, through the spin Hall effect, a z-polarized spin current injected into the liquid metal at y = ±L. The injected spin angular momentum produces an inhomogeneous spin chemical potential and drives liquid-metal motion through the Einstein–de Haas angular-momentum-transfer channel.

Figure 2. Time evolution of the dimensionless EdH-driven velocity profile ˜vx = vx/v0 at L/λ = 10 for (a) the odd counterflow and (b) the even pumping channel. The coor- dinate is ˜y = y/λ, and time is measured in units of λ2/ν with ν = (µ + µr)/ρ.

Figure 3. Spin-mechanical response functions for actuation and parameter extraction. (a) Universal dc pumping response ¯vx/v0 = 1 −tanh(ℓ)/ℓwith ℓ= L/λ. (b) Magnitude and (c) phase of the normalized even-channel admittance Ye(ω)/Ye(0) for L/λ = 0.5, 1, 3, 10. The even channel behaves as a viscous low-pass filter: the liquid follows the spin torque quasistatically for ωL2/ν ≪1, whereas the mean-flow amplitude rolls off and the phase approaches quadrature when viscous momentum diffusion cannot span the channel within one period. Changing the channel width separates the cutoff scale ν/L2 from the spin-to-flow amplitude.

Figure 3 displays this separation between dc gain and viscous filtering. Panel (a) shows that the dc mean flow is suppressed as ¯vx/v0 ≃(L/λ)2/3 when the channel is narrower than the spin-diffusion length, and approaches ¯vx/v0 ≃1 when L ≫λ. Panels (b) and (c) are the fre- quency response of the same actuator. For ωL2/ν ≪1, viscous momentum diffusion equilibrates the whole chan- nel within each period; the mean velocity follows the in- jected spin current with constant magnitude and nearly zero phase shift. For ωL2/ν ≳1, momentum cannot diffuse across the channel during one cycle; the liquid therefore acts as a mechanical low-pass filter, suppressing the mean flow and driving the phase...
